# Insperity, Inc. (NSP) ‚Äî Interrogator Forensic Dossier

*Bundled transcripts: Q1 2025 (2025-04-29), Q2 2025 (2025-08-01), Q3 2025 (2025-11-04), Q4/FY2025 (2026-02-10). Financials: screener scan + FMP TTM through 2026-03-31. Signal type: deep_value. Methodology: EV/GP.*

---

## 1. NARRATIVE ARC

The story across these four quarters is the anatomy of a **margin shock and the management response to it** ‚Äî not a growth story, a damage-control-and-recovery story.

- **Q1 2025 (the shock lands):** The call opens with what management framed as an *inflection* ‚Äî "we achieved an important inflection point, reestablishing positive growth in paid worksite employees" ‚Äî but is immediately overrun by two blows: (1) a tariff/policy-driven collapse in SMB sentiment ("66% of respondents expect the economic climate to have a negative effect on their business this year‚Ä¶ up from only 29% in January"), and (2) a benefits-cost explosion: "benefits costs exceeded our budget by $28 million," with $12M of it an "unprecedented" prior-period claims-runoff adjustment. The full-year benefits-cost guide is raised from 5.0‚Äì6.5% to 6.5‚Äì7.5%. The forward narrative pivots to *pricing realignment by January 2026* and the *Workday strategic partnership* as the growth engine.

- **Q2 2025 (the shock is not transitory):** Benefits costs miss again ("$12 million" over forecast, now pharmacy/GLP-1-led). Management explicitly debates whether this is "a longer-term new normal" and lands on conservatism. The Workday offering is now named ‚Äî **Insperity HRScale** ‚Äî flagship PEO rebranded **HR 360**. The dominant verb shifts from "growth" to "**profitability improvement in 2026**" via "pricing increases, plan design changes and the negotiation of the anticipated renewal of our contract with UHC." The three-lever recovery plan crystallizes here.

- **Q3 2025 (capitulation on the cost trend):** Benefits cost overshoots by $20M; adjusted EPS goes negative (‚àí$0.20). Management concedes "the prevailing view in the industry is that the higher claims trend experienced in 2025 will persist in 2026" ‚Äî and introduces a striking new causal factor: "**the use of AI tools by health care providers**" driving upcoding. HRScale officially rolls out. The 2026 framing hardens into "**financial performance rebound and growth acceleration**."

- **Q4/FY2025 (the pivot to recovery as the whole thesis):** Adjusted EPS ‚àí$0.60; the year closes with a net loss. But the narrative is now entirely forward: a **new multi-year UHC contract** (large-claim pooling cut to $500K, ~2% cost-side reduction effective Jan-1-2026), benefit-plan-design changes, and pricing ‚Äî "**approximately 60% of our current client base are yet to receive applicable pricing upon their renewal dates over the course of the year.**" Management rightsizes (4% of non-sales staff cut, accelerated sales-office consolidation), expands the credit facility ($650M‚Üí$750M, max leverage 3x‚Üí3.75x), and explicitly lowers the 2026 growth bar (‚àí1.5% to +1.5% WSEE). The thesis has fully migrated from "grow" to "**recover margin in 2026, re-accelerate growth in 2027 on HRScale.**"

**Net arc:** A clean, sequential degradation of 2025 results paired with an increasingly specific, increasingly credible recovery plan for 2026. Growth drivers (HR360 net-hiring) were quietly dropped to near-zero; the load-bearing driver became *self-help margin recovery*, with HRScale as a 2027 option.

## 2. CLAIMS vs FINANCIALS (forensic)

## 3. TONE & CONFIDENCE TRAJECTORY

Guidance specificity *increased* over time ‚Äî by Q4 they gave a full-year EBITDA/EPS range and a "60% of book yet to reprice" roadmap, more granular than the hand-wavy Q1 "realign by January." **Reliability: MEDIUM (improving).** The 2025 track record is a string of misses:

- Q2'25 "**fell slightly under the low end**‚Ä¶ by $0.03 and $1M."

This is **not** a sandbagging name; 2025 was genuine serial disappointment as the cost curve kept outrunning their estimate. The credibility upgrade is mechanical: by Q4 they reset to a *low, wide* base, and the Q1'26 actual (adj EPS $1.31) landed cleanly inside the new framework ‚Äî the first sign the new guide is set conservatively. Management's self-described approach ‚Äî "be conservative because‚Ä¶ we want to be sure" (Q1'25) ‚Äî finally appears to be holding the line at the reset level. # Insperity, Inc. (NSP) ‚Äî Interrogator Forensic Dossier

*Bundled transcripts: Q1 2025 (2025-04-29), Q2 2025 (2025-08-01), Q3 2025 (2025-11-04), Q4/FY2025 (2026-02-10). Financials: screener scan + FMP TTM through 2026-03-31. Signal type: deep_value. Methodology: EV/GP.*

---

## 1. NARRATIVE ARC

The story across these four quarters is the anatomy of a **margin shock and the management response to it** ‚Äî not a growth story, a damage-control-and-recovery story.

- **Q1 2025 (the shock lands):** The call opens with what management framed as an *inflection* ‚Äî "we achieved an important inflection point, reestablishing positive growth in paid worksite employees" ‚Äî but is immediately overrun by two blows: (1) a tariff/policy-driven collapse in SMB sentiment ("66% of respondents expect the economic climate to have a negative effect on their business this year‚Ä¶ up from only 29% in January"), and (2) a benefits-cost explosion: "benefits costs exceeded our budget by $28 million," with $12M of it an "unprecedented" prior-period claims-runoff adjustment. The full-year benefits-cost guide is raised from 5.0‚Äì6.5% to 6.5‚Äì7.5%. The forward narrative pivots to *pricing realignment by January 2026* and the *Workday strategic partnership* as the growth engine.

- **Q2 2025 (the shock is not transitory):** Benefits costs miss again ("$12 million" over forecast, now pharmacy/GLP-1-led). Management explicitly debates whether this is "a longer-term new normal" and lands on conservatism. The Workday offering is now named ‚Äî **Insperity HRScale** ‚Äî flagship PEO rebranded **HR 360**. The dominant verb shifts from "growth" to "**profitability improvement in 2026**" via "pricing increases, plan design changes and the negotiation of the anticipated renewal of our contract with UHC." The three-lever recovery plan crystallizes here.

- **Q3 2025 (capitulation on the cost trend):** Benefits cost overshoots by $20M; adjusted EPS goes negative (‚àí$0.20). Management concedes "the prevailing view in the industry is that the higher claims trend experienced in 2025 will persist in 2026" ‚Äî and introduces a striking new causal factor: "**the use of AI tools by health care providers**" driving upcoding. HRScale officially rolls out. The 2026 framing hardens into "**financial performance rebound and growth acceleration**."

- **Q4/FY2025 (the pivot to recovery as the whole thesis):** Adjusted EPS ‚àí$0.60; the year closes with a net loss. But the narrative is now entirely forward: a **new multi-year UHC contract** (large-claim pooling cut to $500K, ~2% cost-side reduction effective Jan-1-2026), benefit-plan-design changes, and pricing ‚Äî "**approximately 60% of our current client base are yet to receive applicable pricing upon their renewal dates over the course of the year.**" Management rightsizes (4% of non-sales staff cut, accelerated sales-office consolidation), expands the credit facility ($650M‚Üí$750M, max leverage 3x‚Üí3.75x), and explicitly lowers the 2026 growth bar (‚àí1.5% to +1.5% WSEE). The thesis has fully migrated from "grow" to "**recover margin in 2026, re-accelerate growth in 2027 on HRScale.**"

**Net arc:** A clean, sequential degradation of 2025 results paired with an increasingly specific, increasingly credible recovery plan for 2026. Growth drivers (HR360 net-hiring) were quietly dropped to near-zero; the load-bearing driver became *self-help margin recovery*, with HRScale as a 2027 option.

# Insperity (NSP) ‚Äî Forensic Dossier

*Transcripts: Q1 2025 (2025-04-29) ‚Üí Q4 2025 (2026-02-10). Financials: screener scan + FMP TTM as of 2026-03-31. Catalyst status cross-checked against the Q1 2026 print reported post-transcript (April/May 2026).*

## 1. NARRATIVE ARC

The story over these four quarters is a single, continuous **deterioration-and-reassurance loop** around one variable management did not control: medical claims cost.

- **Q1 2025** opened on a hopeful inflection ‚Äî *"We achieved an important inflection point, reestablishing positive growth in paid worksite employees"* ‚Äî then immediately pivoted to damage control as benefits costs blew through budget by $28M (`$12M prior-period runoff + $16M current`). Management framed it as possibly *"a shorter-term increase in utilization,"* hedging against calling it a new normal. The forward story was already being relocated to a **future** event: the Workday partnership.
- **Q2 2025** the claim that it might pass faded. Benefits cost trend ran +9.6% YoY; management raised the full-year cost-trend forecast again and leaned harder on the 2026 recovery: *"we remain confident in our outlook for accelerated growth and improved profitability in 2026."* HRScale (the Workday joint product) was formally branded.
- **Q3 2025** was the capitulation quarter. Adjusted EPS went **negative (-$0.20)**, EBITDA collapsed to $10M, and management conceded the cost shock was structural and **industry-wide**: *"the prevailing view in the industry is that the higher claims trend experienced in 2025 will persist in 2026."* New, almost exculpatory drivers appeared ‚Äî *"the use of AI tools by health care providers has emerged as an additional contributor"* (upcoding). The **new UHC contract** (large-claim pooling cut to $500K from 1/1/2026) was announced as the cost-side fix.
- **Q4 2025** completed the pivot from growth-company to **turnaround/margin-recovery** story. The headline became *"stability and long-term value creation,"* a 4%-of-non-sales-staff RIF, accelerated sales-office consolidation, and a credit facility amendment that **raised the max leverage ratio from 3.0x to 3.75x EBITDA** ‚Äî a defensive liquidity move. The growth narrative was explicitly deferred: HR360 margin recovery is "the centerpiece of 2026," and HRScale revenue is a **2027** event (*"I'm obviously really excited about '27 because it's the beginning of serious revenue"*).

**Net arc:** a stable, cash-generative HR-outsourcer was repriced by a sudden, persistent medical-cost shock into a margin-recovery turnaround, with the growth engine (HRScale) pushed out to 2027. The drivers quietly dropped: the early-2025 "return to WSEE growth" thesis is gone ‚Äî FY26 is now guided to **-1.5% to +1.5% WSEE growth**.

## 2. CLAIMS vs FINANCIALS (forensic)

| Management claim | Quarter | What the financials show | Verdict |

| "improved profitability in 2026" / margin recovery | Q2‚ÄìQ4 '25 | FY25 NI **-$7M** (EPS -$0.18) vs $91M (2024) and $171M (2023). ## 3. TONE & CONFIDENCE TRAJECTORY

The shift from **offensive (growth/investment) to defensive (efficiency/cost/macro)** language is unmistakable and monotonic across the four calls. ## 5. ANALYST PRESSURE POINTS

- **"Will pricing stick / will attrition spike?"** (Jeff Martin, Q1'25) ‚Äî management deflected to "strategically selected accounts" and "we've managed this many times." The Q4'25 admission that **attrition ran higher** ("company-initiated nonrenewals," retention down to ~83%) validated the analyst worry.

bull_thesis: Insperity is a professional-employer organization, or PEO (a firm that co-employs a small business's staff and runs its payroll, benefits, and HR compliance), and at about $36 a share the market is treating it as a permanently broken business when the evidence points to a confirmed earnings low rather than a lasting decline. The company holds more cash than debt, by roughly $125 million. Its 2025 reported earnings were wiped out (a net loss of about $7 million, or $0.18 a share) by a single outside force: an industry-wide spike in medical-claims costs, driven by GLP-1 and specialty-drug spending plus AI-assisted billing by healthcare providers, which ran over management's budget by $28 million, $12 million, and $20 million across the first three quarters of 2025. The durable economics never broke. Return on invested capital (the profit the business earns on the money tied up in it) held at 16.5% even at the loss-making bottom, client retention rose to about 83% from roughly 81%, and the company kept paying $90 million in dividends straight through the loss year. The recovery is now showing up in results, not just in promises: first-quarter 2026 adjusted earnings came in at $1.31 a share (above the midpoint of guidance), adjusted operating earnings (adjusted EBITDA, a measure of cash profit before interest, taxes, and accounting charges) were $103 million (up about 1% from a year earlier), the year-over-year gross-profit decline narrowed from down 21% in the fourth quarter of 2025 to down just 3%, and the benefits-cost trend slowed from roughly 9% to 5%. Three self-help levers are stacking up: first, a new UnitedHealthcare contract cut the large-claim pooling limit from $1 million to $500,000, structurally capping catastrophic-claim risk, and management says its earnings benefit is weighted toward the second half of 2026 and sits above current Wall Street models; second, repricing and more selective client choices on the roughly 60% of the book that has not yet renewed at the new rates; and third, plan-design changes plus a 4% cut to non-sales headcount. Full-year 2026 guidance is adjusted operating earnings of $170 million to $230 million and adjusted earnings of $1.60 to $2.60 a share, with consensus forward earnings growth of about 36%. The clearest signal is from insiders: 23 open-market purchases (572,566 shares) against 7 sales (46,256 shares) means insiders bought roughly 12 times the dollars they sold, buying directly into the trough. There is a free option on top: HRScale, a partnership with Workday that moves Insperity up-market into accounts of 150 to 5,000 employees (averaging 400 to 700 worksite employees, versus 25 to 30 historically), with early clients onboarded in March 2026 and meaningful revenue expected to inflect in 2027 as the investment cost tapers. If gross profit per worksite employee recovers toward its historical level of more than $400 a month from $332 in the first quarter of 2026, even on a flat client base, normalized earnings move back toward the high-$2s or low-$3s a share and operating earnings toward the upper half of guidance, which would be a re-rating of more than 40% from $36, cushioned by the $125 million net-cash position. This depends on the second-half UnitedHealthcare benefit landing as guided, repricing not driving client losses beyond plan, and no fresh leg up in medical-claims costs.
bear_thesis: The stock is genuinely cheap, but the upside is thinner than it looks because the main catalyst (the UnitedHealthcare contract) is already in effect and the client base is structurally shrinking. Management missed its own guidance four quarters running in 2025 (first quarter below range, second quarter under the low end, third quarter below the ranges) and kept re-sizing its benefits-cost forecast (from 5% to 6.5%, then 6.5% to 7.5%, then up another 75 to 100 basis points, then roughly 9% actual), for a 43% beat rate (3 of 7 quarters tracked). The whole point of a PEO is managing benefits risk for small businesses, and they could not forecast their own single largest cost line for a full year; one in-line first quarter of 2026 does not erase that. Worse, the growth engine is broken independent of benefits: the company guided worksite-employee growth of negative 1.5% to positive 1.5% for 2026 (in other words, flat to shrinking), new-client worksite employees fell 7% year over year in the first quarter of 2026, and the client base has seen near-zero net hiring for three straight years. The first-quarter 2026 margin recovery is partly survivorship: management concedes that the biggest driver was client mix (lower-profit clients leaving, higher-profit ones staying), not durable pricing power, and earnings are being flattered by newly capitalizing Workday costs that used to be expensed ($5 million capitalized in the first quarter of 2026). The full-year 2026 earnings range is enormous, $1.60 to $2.60 a share, a $1.00 spread, which signals low conviction. HRScale has absorbed two years of cash through the income statement ($58 million in 2025 alone) with zero revenue, and its payoff date keeps sliding to 2027, on a Workday relationship that is now navigating a leadership change. Structurally, a co-employment model that bills per worksite employee is exposed to AI-driven cuts in white-collar headcount among its small-business clients. There is also valuation risk: PEO peers have already de-rated, with TriNet (TNET) trading at about 7 times free cash flow and Barrett (BBSI) at about 10 times; Insperity's net margin of about 0% is weaker than TriNet's 3.1%, so a peer-multiple anchor does not support a large re-rating off a still-depressed earnings base. If the second-half-weighted UnitedHealthcare benefit disappoints, the four-miss credibility record reasserts itself and the stock re-tests its low. What would confirm this bearish view: a renewed rise in benefits costs in the second or third quarter of 2026, worksite employees printing the low end (negative 1.5%), or HRScale's 2027 revenue getting pushed back again.
consensus_delta: Here is where we differ from the market. The deep-value question is whether the market is pricing a terminal decline into a company with intact, recovering earnings power, and the honest answer here is: only partly. The genuine error the market made was extrapolating the 2025 medical-claims spike as permanent damage to margins, but the first-quarter 2026 print (benefits trend falling from 9% to 5%, the gross-profit decline narrowing from down 21% to down 3%, adjusted operating earnings up about 1% year over year) shows it was an outside, recovering shock on a franchise whose return on invested capital held at 16.5% throughout. The specific point management flagged as under-appreciated: the new UnitedHealthcare benefit is, in their words, more weighted to the second half than some analyst estimates assume, meaning second-half 2026 numbers may be too low. That said, the larger reality is that PEO peers have already de-rated (TriNet at about 7 times free cash flow) and the live analyst target range has compressed to roughly $39.50 to $47.50 (a Hold consensus), well below the stale $52 figure in our data, so the market is not badly mispriced; it has already marked the stock toward fair value. The remaining edge is narrow: the second-half UnitedHealthcare benefit plus the roughly 60% of the book still to reprice could drive a beat, but the lead catalyst is already live and the client base is shrinking (negative 1.5% to positive 1.5% growth), which caps the re-rating.
forcing_function: These are the specific events that could force a re-rating. First, second-quarter 2026 earnings (around late July or early August 2026, roughly 44 days out from our data) give the first read on whether the second-half-weighted UnitedHealthcare benefit and the repriced 60% of the book are translating into the margin step-up management guided; a clean beat here confirms the recovery and is the primary trigger. Second, third-quarter 2026 results (around November 2026) are the quarter when the UnitedHealthcare reimbursement weighting and full repricing should peak, making it the cleanest single proof point for full-year 2026 reaching the upper half of the $170 million to $230 million operating-earnings guide. Third, the 2027 HRScale revenue inflection is the longer-dated growth trigger, though it has been pushed back repeatedly and we hold low conviction on the timing. There is no activist, proxy fight, or asset sale to force the issue here; this is a self-help operational recovery, not an event-driven special situation. The re-rating has to be earned quarter by quarter on margin proof, not forced by a corporate event.
valley_of_death: The risky window over the next three to nine months is real but cushioned by the balance sheet. First, the UnitedHealthcare benefit is weighted to the back half of the year: Insperity pays the higher fixed $500,000-pooling premium evenly across all of 2026 while the claim reimbursements concentrate in the second half, so if medical use reaccelerates in the second or third quarter (the 2025 pattern), the recovery story cracks before the second-half benefit lands, and the four-miss credibility record reasserts itself. Second, adjusted cash fell to just $36 million as of March 31, 2026 (a seasonal working-capital low), which looks alarming on its own, though net cash is still positive $125 million on the full balance sheet and $330 million remains available on the credit facility (amended to $750 million of capacity, with the leverage cap raised from 3 times to 3.75 times, meaning they built liquidity headroom as a precaution). Third, the tax rate is volatile: it was 41% in the first quarter of 2026 versus 29% before, because the depressed stock price shrank the deductions tied to vesting stock compensation, a self-reinforcing drag on reported earnings as long as the price stays low. Fourth, the client base is shrinking into a weak small-business hiring backdrop; a recession or AI-driven cuts to small-business headcount would hit the per-worksite-employee revenue model directly. There is no looming debt maturity (the facility runs to December 2028). The dividend (about $90 million a year) is well covered by the recovering operating earnings and is not at risk of being cut, so there is no forced-seller dynamic from a dividend cut. In short, this is an earnings-volatility risk window, not a solvency one.
positioning_washout: There is limited forced-selling pressure here, which cuts both ways. Insperity is a profitable dividend payer with a strong balance sheet, so there is no dividend cut on the table to trigger a mass exit by income-focused holders (the classic deep-value flush is absent because the dividend is intact). The 2025 selloff has already happened (the stock sits 39% up its 52-week range, down 1% versus its 200-day average), and insiders bought the dip aggressively (23 open-market purchases, 572,566 shares), meaning the strong hands already stepped in at the bottom. The smart-money gauge sits at 31 out of 100 and institutional flow at 60 out of 100, both middling. Because there is no overhang of forced selling left to clear, there is no capitulation flush to wait for in the usual sense; the washout already occurred in 2025. That argues for a watch-and-wait-on-execution stance (wait for the second-half UnitedHealthcare benefit to confirm) rather than waiting for a mechanical flush that the intact dividend makes unlikely.
moderator_conclusion: Our call is to keep this on the watchlist for confirmation, with moderate conviction (3 out of 5). This is a genuine net-cash franchise coming off a confirmed, externally-driven earnings low. The first-quarter 2026 print shows the medical-claims shock was cyclical, not structural, and the insider buying (12 times more dollars bought than sold into the trough) is the strongest tell in the file. The underlying value case is strong: return on invested capital held at 16.5% through a reported-loss year, the competitive advantage (the switching costs that keep PEO clients in place) is narrow but stable rather than eroding, the longer-term threat from AI cutting headcount is manageable (HRScale moves the business up-market, and AI also helps spawn new small businesses), and the balance sheet is solid with the dividend intact. On the value merits alone, that earns a conviction of 4. But the catalyst picture pulls the action score down: the main recovery catalyst (the new UnitedHealthcare contract) is already live, so that part of the re-rating is partly spent, and the market has already marked the stock toward fair value (a live target range of about $39.50 to $47.50, a Hold, not the stale $52). Against our current investing backdrop, which favors hard-dated catalysts arriving inside a defined window and leans toward structural special situations in thinly-covered distressed or forced-seller corners of the market, an already-fired, self-help operational turnaround on a flat-to-shrinking client base is exactly the kind of setup we currently deprioritize. It is not a thinly-covered distressed or forced-seller special situation, and its remaining upside hinges on the same medical-cost driver that hurt it in 2025, so its resolution does not depend on an independent driver. The risk and reward are favorable on the downside (the net-cash floor caps the fall to roughly $33 to $35) but the base-case upside to about $42 of fair value is modest, around 16%. The right posture is to watch and wait: do not chase at $36 with the UnitedHealthcare benefit already in motion. Wait for the second-quarter 2026 print (around early August) to confirm the second-half-weighted UnitedHealthcare benefit and the second-half margin step-up are real. A clean second-quarter beat with the benefits trend holding at or below 5% would turn this into an entry; a renewed rise in benefits costs would re-test the low and re-arm the four-miss credibility concern. Our base-case sum-of-the-parts fair value (valuing the business and its net cash separately) is about $42 a share: 8 times operating earnings on roughly $200 million, plus $3.30 a share of net cash, plus about $1 a share for the HRScale option.
sop_breakdown: Here is the base-case build, on roughly 38 million shares, valuing the pieces separately. Operating value: full-year 2026 adjusted operating earnings at the midpoint of about $200 million, at roughly 8 times enterprise value to operating earnings (a recovery multiple between the de-rated TriNet level of about 7 times and Barrett at about 10 times; Insperity earns the half-turn premium over TriNet for its net-cash balance sheet and the UnitedHealthcare $500,000 tail-cap, but not the 25 to 30 times that ADP and Paychex command, since those firms out-scale it on technology), which gives about $1.6 billion of enterprise value, or roughly $38 a share of operating value. Net-cash overlay: plus $125 million ($555 million of cash minus $430 million of debt as of March 31, 2026), a genuine add-back since the dividend was never cut and there is no pending distribution, or about $3.30 a share. HRScale option: a real up-market growth call but not yet generating revenue until 2027, credited modestly at about $1 a share in the base case. That sums to about $38 of operations plus $3.30 of net cash plus $1 for HRScale, or roughly $42 a share. As a cross-check against earnings: normalized adjusted earnings recovering toward about $2.50 to $2.80 a share (between the full-year 2026 guidance midpoint of $2.10 and a fuller 2027 recovery) at about 15 times comes to $40 to $42, which is consistent. This is anchored to the de-rated PEO peer set (TriNet at about 7 times free cash flow), not to the stale 25-to-30-times large-cap payroll multiples.
risk_reward: The downside to a break is roughly $33 to $35 (the bear-case fair value, about 5% to 9% below the $36.22 price, where the net-cash floor and low-end operating earnings cap the fall). The upside to fair value is about $42 in the base case (up 16%) and about $50 in the bull case (up 38%). That is a risk-to-reward of roughly 1.6-to-1 up to 4-to-1 to fair value; the downside is well protected by net cash, but the base-case upside is modest because the lead catalyst (UnitedHealthcare) is already live and partly priced in, and the client base is flat to shrinking.
peer_comps_note: Our read on the peer comparison is that Insperity looks roughly in line with its true peers, not at a deep discount. We anchor to the de-rated PEO peer set, not to stale large-cap payroll multiples. The closest peer, TriNet (TNET), trades at about 7 times free cash flow; Barrett (BBSI) at about 10 times. ADP and Paychex trade at 25 to 30 times, but they out-scale Insperity on technology and platform and are not the right anchor. Insperity's net margin of about 0% (its current trough) is weaker than TriNet's 3.1%, so against today's de-rated group, Insperity at the earnings trough screens roughly in line rather than at a deep company-specific discount. The re-rating case therefore depends on Insperity's own margin recovery (gross profit per worksite employee climbing from $332 back toward its historical level of more than $400), not on closing a stale peer-multiple gap. We used 8 times enterprise value to operating earnings (TriNet's roughly 7 times plus a half-turn for net cash and the UnitedHealthcare $500,000 tail-cap) as the base anchor, deliberately not a pre-shock 12-to-15 times, which would overstate the apparent discount.
interrogator_dossier:
# A deep dive on management and the setup ‚Äî Insperity, Inc. (NSP)

*A forensic review of five consecutive quarterly calls (first quarter 2025 through first quarter 2026), cross-referenced against the financial trajectory. Sector: industrials (commercial services / professional-employer organizations). Signal type: deep value.*

---

## 1. The narrative arc

The story over these five calls is a controlled demolition of a once-pristine PEO margin model, followed by a scripted recovery, and the arc is unusually clean because management never hid the wound; they just kept re-sizing it.

- **First quarter 2025 (April 29):** The call opens on what was supposed to be a growth-reacceleration story. Management had "achieved an important inflection point, reestablishing positive growth in paid worksite employees." That thesis is immediately overrun by two shocks introduced in the same call: a tariff- and policy-driven collapse in small-business sentiment ("66% of respondents expect the economic climate to have a negative effect... up from only 29% in January"), and the first appearance of the benefits-cost problem, with first-quarter benefits exceeding budget by $28 million ($12 million of prior-period claims runoff plus $16 million current), described as "an unprecedented occurrence." The Workday partnership (HRScale) is introduced here as the forward growth engine.
- **Second quarter 2025 (August 1):** Benefits cost is now the whole story. The second quarter exceeded forecast by another $12 million, driven by GLP-1 and specialty-pharmacy spending. The tone pivots hard from growth to margin defense: pricing increases, plan-design changes, and "the negotiation of the anticipated renewal of our contract with UHC." Growth is explicitly deprioritized: "we're not anticipating much support from the client growth."
- **Third quarter 2025 (November 4):** The bottom. Adjusted earnings of negative $0.20 a share, adjusted operating earnings of just $10 million, a benefits overage of $20 million. Management concedes the trend is structural, not a spike: "the prevailing view in the industry is that the higher claims trend experienced in 2025 will persist in 2026." A new outside culprit appears: AI-driven upcoding by providers ("the use of AI tools by health care providers has emerged as an additional contributor"). HRScale is formally launched.
- **Fourth quarter 2025 (February 10):** The capitulation quarter dressed up as a turnaround. Adjusted earnings of negative $0.60 a share, adjusted operating earnings of negative $13 million. But the narrative flips to recovery: "measurable margin recovery" in the fall sales campaign, a new UnitedHealthcare contract (pooling limit cut from $1 million to $500,000), a 4% cut to non-sales staff, and a credit facility amended (capacity from $650 million to $750 million, maximum leverage from 3 times to 3.75 times). Growth guidance for 2026 is set at negative 1.5% to positive 1.5%, in other words, shrinking.
- **First quarter 2026 (April 30):** The proof-of-recovery quarter, and it largely delivers. Adjusted earnings of $1.31 a share (above the midpoint), adjusted operating earnings of $103 million (up about 1% year over year), the gross-profit decline narrowed to down 3% versus down 21% in the fourth quarter. The benefits-cost trend slowed from roughly 9% to 5%. The story is now explicitly a three-year plan: year one margin recovery, year two balanced growth, year three (2027) HRScale revenue inflection. AI is reframed from threat (provider upcoding) to opportunity (Insperity's own AI initiatives).

**Drivers introduced and dropped:** Workday/HRScale was introduced as a growth catalyst in the first quarter of 2025 and survived the whole arc, but its payoff was pushed back repeatedly, from "margin builder in the future" (first quarter 2025) to early clients onboarding in March 2026 with meaningful revenue not until 2027. The original 2025 growth-reacceleration thesis was quietly abandoned; "growth" was redefined from headcount expansion to margin recovery.

---

## 2. Claims versus financials (forensic)

| Management claim | Quarter | Hard financials | Assessment |
|---|---|---|---|
| "Reestablishing positive growth in paid worksite employees" | Q1 2025 | Full-year 2025 revenue up 3.5%; worksite-employee growth ended 2025 at about plus 1%; 2026 guided negative 1.5% to positive 1.5% | Overstated; growth never materialized, and the base is now shrinking |
| "Higher-than-expected benefits costs... an unprecedented occurrence" | Q1 2025 | 2024 net income $91 million to 2025 net income negative $7 million; earnings per share $2.39 to negative $0.18; net margin negative 0.1% | Confirmed and then some; this single line item wiped out all reported earnings |
| Margin recovery "on track"; "step-up in gross profit key drivers" | Q4 2025 | Q1 2026 gross-profit decline narrowed to down 3% (versus down 21% in Q4 2025); benefits trend 9% to 5%; adjusted operating earnings $103 million (up about 1% year over year) | Corroborated; the clearest case where the narrative matched the numbers |
| Capital return / shareholder-friendly | All | Paid $90 million in dividends in 2025, bought back $19 million of stock; net cash $125 million (cash $555 million versus debt $430 million) as of March 31, 2026 | Confirmed; they kept the dividend through a loss year and the balance sheet remains strong |
| Return on capital / returns quality | ‚Äî | Return on invested capital 16.5% even at the trough; return on equity of negative 1,303% is a denominator artifact (near-zero equity), not economic | Returns base intact; the return-on-equity figure is noise, not signal |
| HRScale "engine for growth," "significant catalyst" | Q3 2025 / Q1 2026 | Zero revenue contribution to date; $58 million invested in 2025 alone, all through the income statement; meaningful revenue is a 2027 event | Story richer than the numbers; a real option, but unmonetized and dated to 2027 |
| Forward earnings growth | ‚Äî | Consensus forward earnings growth plus 36.1%; full-year 2026 guide of $1.60 to $2.60 adjusted earnings, $170 million to $230 million adjusted operating earnings | Recovery is real, but the range is enormous (a $1.00 spread in earnings per share), which signals low conviction |

**Where the story is richer than the numbers justify:** HRScale. It has absorbed real cash for two years with no revenue, and the payoff date keeps sliding to 2027. **Where the story is, if anything, too modest:** the actual margin-recovery execution in the first quarter of 2026 is better than the beaten-down narrative implied; management under-promised on the trough, and the recovery is ahead of the bears' fears.

---

## 3. Tone and confidence trajectory

**Trajectory: declining through the third quarter of 2025, then turning up into the first quarter of 2026 (V-shaped).**

- **Q1 2025 ‚Äî defensive and surprised:** "We are obviously disappointed by the emergence of higher benefits costs." Offensive growth language already yielding to cost language.
- **Q2 2025 ‚Äî defensive and hedging:** "I personally don't get too caught up in what the actual specific quarterly trend is." Repeated externalization ("not unique to Insperity," "commonplace to address current market conditions").
- **Q3 2025 ‚Äî defensive, at the bottom:** the AI-upcoding explanation is the tell, management reaching for a novel external cause to explain a fourth consecutive miss. Confidence visibly low; the guidance range absurdly wide (full-year adjusted earnings of $0.84 to $1.47).
- **Q4 2025 ‚Äî pivoting, scripted confidence:** "we accomplished this key objective," "decisive actions." A deliberate reframing toward a three-year plan and a 40th-anniversary narrative, a classic management move to change the subject from the trailing results.
- **Q1 2026 ‚Äî genuinely more confident, and earned:** "We are pleased with our Q1 earnings results." For the first time in the arc, the confidence is backed by a beat (adjusted earnings above the midpoint, operating earnings up about 1% year over year). Guidance specificity returns.

The shift runs from offensive to defensive (first through third quarters of 2025) to cautiously offensive (first quarter of 2026). The defensiveness was warranted; the renewed confidence is, for once, supported by a printed number.

---

## 4. Guidance credibility

**Reliability: low through 2025, improving to medium by the first quarter of 2026.**

Management missed its own guidance four quarters running in 2025: the first quarter "fell below our guidance range," the second "fell slightly under the low end," the third "fell below our forecasted ranges," and the full-year benefits trend blew through every successive range they set ("5% to 6.5%" to "6.5% to 7.5%" to "75 to 100 basis points higher" to roughly 9% actual). The beat rate of 3 of 7 quarters (43%) confirms a sub-coin-flip delivery record. There is no pattern of lowballing guidance in 2025; they genuinely could not forecast their own largest cost line, which is the single most damning credibility fact in this file.

The redemption: the first quarter of 2026 came in above the midpoint of guidance, and benefits cost (5% versus the roughly 9% run-rate) beat. One quarter does not reset a four-quarter miss streak, but the direction of credibility is now positive. Crucially, management flagged that the UnitedHealthcare contract benefit is weighted to the second half ("more back-end loaded than... we have maybe clearly communicated... some earnings estimates that are out there on the analyst side"), a setup that either becomes a second-half beat or a fresh miss. Score: medium, on probation.

---

## 5. Analyst pressure points

- **"Is this benefits trend permanent or transitory?"** ‚Äî pushed every quarter (Polkowitz at JPMorgan, Marcon). Management's answer migrated from "unprecedented spike" (first quarter) to "will persist in 2026" (third quarter); the analysts who pressed on permanence were right, and management was slow.
- **HRScale/Workday profitability and timing** ‚Äî Jeff Martin (ROTH) and Polkowitz repeatedly pressed "how should we think about profitability" and "is the $150M aggregate investment still right." Management consistently non-answered ("we haven't locked that down yet," "we don't have a better number right now"). Two years in, the unit economics are still undisclosed, a persistent deflection.
- **Pricing versus client-loss risk** ‚Äî analysts worried that higher pricing would drive churn. Management insisted retention would hold; full-year 2025 retention came in at about 83% (versus about 81% prior), so this one held up.
- **Workday leadership change** (fourth quarter 2025, Marcon) ‚Äî flagged as a relationship risk; management waved it off. An unresolved dependency on an outside partner.

The topic analysts pushed on that became bad news: the permanence of the benefits trend. The topic they keep pushing that management still won't quantify: HRScale's economics.

---

## 6. Red flags and green flags

Red flags:
- **Four consecutive guidance misses in 2025**, with a cost line management repeatedly mis-forecast. The core PEO value proposition is managing benefits risk for small businesses, and they failed to price their own risk for a full year.
- **2025 reported net loss (negative $7 million, negative $0.18 a share)** and trailing-twelve-month diluted earnings of negative $0.67 a share; earnings power is genuinely impaired, not merely optically cheap.
- **Shrinking base:** worksite employees guided negative 1.5% to positive 1.5% for 2026; new-client worksite employees down 7% year over year in the first quarter of 2026; three straight years of "very low single-digit net gain" in the client base. The growth engine is stalled independent of benefits.
- **Tax-rate volatility** (41% in the first quarter of 2026 versus 29% prior) from the depressed stock price reducing stock-compensation deductions, a self-reinforcing drag on reported earnings while the stock stays low.
- **HRScale is two years of cash burn through the income statement ($58 million in 2025) for a 2027 payoff**, a real option, but the date keeps sliding right.

Green flags:
- **Net cash of $125 million** (cash $555 million versus total debt $430 million), a genuine add-back to value and a downside cushion; the dividend was never cut through the loss year.
- **First-quarter 2026 margin-recovery beat** ‚Äî benefits trend 9% to 5%, gross-profit decline from down 21% to down 3%, adjusted operating earnings up about 1% year over year. The recovery thesis is showing up in results.
- **Real insider conviction** ‚Äî open-market only: 23 purchases (572,566 shares) versus 7 sales (46,256 shares). Insiders bought roughly 12 times the dollar value they sold, into the trough. This is the strongest single bullish tell in the file.
- **New UnitedHealthcare contract structurally de-risks the tail** ‚Äî the pooling limit cut from $1 million to $500,000 caps catastrophic-claim exposure, and the benefit is weighted toward the second half of 2026.
- **Cost discipline** ‚Äî operating expenses down about 3% in 2025, with further cuts targeted and $9 million of restructuring already taken in the first quarter of 2026.

---

## 7. Hidden signals

- **The AI framing flips 180 degrees.** In the third quarter of 2025, AI is a cost villain, with providers using AI to upcode claims against Insperity. By the first quarter of 2026, AI is an Insperity opportunity ("our AI initiatives," "we're on the front end of AI"). Watch this: it is a tell that management reframes outside forces to fit each quarter's narrative. The genuinely interesting subtext (third quarter 2025, Allison) is that AI-driven white-collar layoffs could shrink Insperity's own addressable headcount, a structural risk to a model that bills per worksite employee.
- **"Success penalty" language** (first quarter 2026): management admits that clients it "helped grow" outgrow the small-business PEO and leave; HRScale is explicitly the retention fix for graduating clients, not just new growth. That reframes HRScale from offense to defense against structural churn up-market.
- **Capitalization shift:** beginning in the third quarter of 2025, Workday costs crossed the software-capitalization threshold ($5 million capitalized in the first quarter of 2026). Watch for earnings being flattered by capitalizing what was previously expensed, a quiet decline in earnings quality as the recovery is sold.
- **Client mix is doing the heavy lifting on margin** (first quarter 2026, Allison): "the biggest impact is the client mix," meaning lower-profit clients leaving and higher-profit ones staying. The margin recovery is partly survivorship of the shrinking base, not pure pricing power.

---

## 8. Capital-allocation verdict (for the director)

**Classification: pivoting (a margin-recovery turnaround off a confirmed earnings low), with a stable balance sheet underneath.**

The single most important thing the director must weigh: this is a self-help margin-recovery story whose first-quarter 2026 print is genuinely better than the bear case, but it sits on a client base that is structurally non-growing (currently shrinking), and the load-bearing recovery catalyst (the UnitedHealthcare contract) is already live and partly in the price. The question is no longer "will it survive" (the net-cash balance sheet and the first-quarter 2026 beat answer that) but "will margin recover to historical levels (gross profit per worksite employee back toward more than $400 a month) on a flat-to-shrinking client base." If yes, full-year 2026 adjusted operating earnings land in the upper half above $200 million and earnings normalize toward the high-$2s or low-$3s a share, which is meaningful upside from $36. If the second-half-weighted UnitedHealthcare benefit disappoints, the four-miss credibility record reasserts and the stock re-tests the low.

**Economic moat ‚Äî narrow, of the switching-costs type, and stable rather than eroding.** The PEO model has genuine switching costs (co-employment, benefits administration, compliance entanglement), and return on invested capital held at 16.5% even at the reported-loss trough; the durable earnings base did not break, it was margin-compressed by an outside medical-cost shock. The gross-margin contraction (to 13.2%, less than 2.5 standard deviations below its historical peak) is cyclical and external (a medical-claims spike), not competitive-moat erosion, and the first quarter of 2026 already shows it reflecting. This is not a high-but-falling-returns eroding moat; returns were stable, and margins took a one-time hit that is recovering. The moat is narrow because ADP and Paychex out-scale on technology and the small-business base churns (the "success penalty").

**Secular threat ‚Äî manageable, not terminal.** The relevant structural force is AI-driven deflation in white-collar headcount: a co-employment model that bills per worksite employee is exposed if AI structurally shrinks small-business payrolls. But management credibly notes no evidence of AI job replacement in its base yet, AI historically spawns new small-business formation (a partial offset), and HRScale moves Insperity up-market to larger, stickier accounts. The medical-cost shock that caused the cheapness is cyclical and company-specific, not secular; this is a fear-driven price on a recovering franchise, not a structurally-shrinking value trap. The genuine caution is the flat client base, which caps the re-rating ceiling.

Credibility: 3 out of 5. Trajectory: turning. Moat: narrow. Moat trend: stable. Secular threat: manageable.
